title: Mock Final â€” Variant A (Predictable Standard)

problems.title: CramĂ©r--Rao Lower Bound
problems.prompt: State the Cram\'er--Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) for the variance of an unbiased estimator $T = T(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ of $g(\theta)$. Then state \textbf{one} regularity condition required for the bound to hold.

parts.label: (answer)
parts.modelAnswer:
If $T(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ is an unbiased estimator of $g(\theta)$ (so $E_\theta[T] = g(\theta)$ for all $\theta$), then under the regularity conditions
\[
\mathrm{Var}_\theta(T) \;\geq\; \frac{[g'(\theta)]^2}{n\, I(\theta)},
\]
where $I(\theta) = E_\theta\!\left[\left(\tfrac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\log f(X;\theta)\right)^2\right]$ is the Fisher information in a single observation. In the special case $g(\theta) = \theta$, the bound becomes $\mathrm{Var}_\theta(T) \geq 1/[n I(\theta)]$.

**One regularity condition.** Any one of the following:

  - (A.1) The support $\{x : f(x;\theta) > 0\}$ does not depend on $\theta$.
  - (A.2) Differentiation under the integral sign is valid: $\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta}\int W(x) f(x;\theta) \,dx = \int W(x) \frac{\partial}{\partial\theta} f(x;\theta) \,dx$.
  - (A.3) $\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta} \log f(x;\theta)$ exists and is finite for every $x$ in the support.

**Answer.** $\mathrm{Var}_\theta(T) \geq [g'(\theta)]^2 / [n I(\theta)]$, with one regularity condition such as ``support of $f$ does not depend on $\theta$.''}

% --- A2 ---

problems.title: Neyman--Pearson Lemma
problems.prompt: State the Neyman--Pearson Lemma. Be sure to specify the testing setup (simple vs.\ composite hypotheses) and the form of the optimal test.

parts.label: (answer)
parts.modelAnswer:
**Setup.** Simple null vs.\ simple alternative: $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ vs.\ $H_1: \theta = \theta_1$, with joint densities $f_0$ and $f_1$.

**Statement.** For any constant $k \geq 0$, the test $\delta^*$ defined by

  - Reject $H_0$ if $f_1(\mathbf{x}) > k\, f_0(\mathbf{x})$,
  - Do not reject if $f_1(\mathbf{x}) < k\, f_0(\mathbf{x})$,
  - Either decision is allowed when $f_1(\mathbf{x}) = k\, f_0(\mathbf{x})$,

is most powerful at its level: for any other test $\delta$ with $\alpha(\delta) \leq \alpha(\delta^*)$, we have $\beta(\delta) \geq \beta(\delta^*)$ (equivalently, $\pi(\theta_1; \delta) \leq \pi(\theta_1; \delta^*)$).

**Answer.** The likelihood-ratio test that rejects when $f_1/f_0 > k$ minimizes Type II error among all level-$\alpha(\delta^*)$ tests.}

% --- A3 ---

problems.title: Sufficient Statistics
problems.prompt: Define what it means for a statistic $T = \varphi(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ to be \emph{sufficient} for $\theta$, and state the \textbf{factorization criterion}.

parts.label: (answer)
parts.modelAnswer:
A statistic $T = \varphi(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ is _sufficient_ for $\theta$ if the conditional distribution of $(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ given $T = t$ does not depend on $\theta$.

**Factorization criterion.** $T$ is sufficient for $\theta$ if and only if the joint density/pmf factors as
\[
f_n(\mathbf{x}; \theta) = u(\mathbf{x}) \cdot v(\varphi(\mathbf{x}), \theta)
\]
for some non-negative functions $u$ (free of $\theta$) and $v$ (depending on the data only through $T$).

**Answer.** Sufficiency means the conditional distribution of the data given $T$ is free of $\theta$; factorization: $f_n(\mathbf{x};\theta) = u(\mathbf{x}) v(\varphi(\mathbf{x}),\theta)$.}

% --- A4 ---

problems.title: Test/CI Duality
problems.prompt:
In 2--3 sentences, describe the duality between hypothesis tests and confidence intervals. In particular, explain how a level-$\alpha_0$ test of $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ gives rise to a $(1-\alpha_0)$ confidence set for $\theta$.

% =====================================================================
% PART B: Mid-Length Problems
% =====================================================================
\section*{Part B --- Mid-Length Problems \hfill (30 points total)}

parts.label: (answer)
parts.modelAnswer:
For each $\theta_0 \in \Omega$, let $\mathcal{A}(\theta_0)$ be the acceptance region of a level-$\alpha_0$ test of $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$. Define the random set
\[
\mathcal{S}(\mathbf{X}) = \{\theta_0 : \mathbf{X} \in \mathcal{A}(\theta_0)\}.
\]
Then $\mathcal{S}(\mathbf{X})$ is a $(1 - \alpha_0)$ confidence set for $\theta$, because $P_\theta(\theta \in \mathcal{S}(\mathbf{X})) = P_\theta(\mathbf{X} \in \mathcal{A}(\theta)) \geq 1 - \alpha_0$. Conversely, given a confidence set $\mathcal{S}$, the test ``reject $H_0:\theta=\theta_0$ iff $\theta_0 \notin \mathcal{S}(\mathbf{X})$'' has level $\alpha_0$.

**Answer.** The $(1-\alpha_0)$ CI is the set of null values $\theta_0$ that the level-$\alpha_0$ test would NOT reject; equivalently, invert the acceptance region.}

% =====================================================================
% PART B
% =====================================================================
\section*{Part B --- Mid-Length Problems}

problems.title: MLE for the Exponential, Delta Method, and CI
problems.prompt:
Let $X_1, \dots, X_n$ be a random sample from the exponential distribution with rate parameter $\theta > 0$, i.e.\ density
\[
f(x \mid \theta) = \theta e^{-\theta x}, \qquad x > 0.
\]
Recall that $E_\theta[X_1] = 1/\theta$ and $\mathrm{Var}_\theta(X_1) = 1/\theta^2$.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) $\widehat{\theta}_n$ of $\theta$. Show your work.
parts.modelAnswer:
The likelihood and log-likelihood are
\[
L_n(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^n \theta e^{-\theta X_i} = \theta^n \exp\!\Big(-\theta \textstyle\sum_i X_i\Big), \qquad
\ell_n(\theta) = n \log\theta - \theta \sum_{i=1}^n X_i.
\]
Score: $\dot{\ell}_n(\theta) = n/\theta - \sum X_i$. Setting equal to zero gives $\theta = n/\sum X_i = 1/\bar{X}_n$. The second derivative is $-n/\theta^2 < 0$, so this is the unique maximum.

**Answer.** $\widehat{\theta}_n = 1/\bar{X}_n$.}

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Show that $\widehat{\theta}_n$ is a consistent estimator of $\theta$. State clearly which limit theorem(s) you are using.
parts.modelAnswer:
By the Weak Law of Large Numbers, $\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{P} E_\theta[X_1] = 1/\theta$. The map $g(t) = 1/t$ is continuous on $(0, \infty)$, so by the continuous mapping theorem,
\[
\widehat{\theta}_n = 1/\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{P} 1/(1/\theta) = \theta.
\]

**Answer.** $\widehat{\theta}_n \xrightarrow{P} \theta$ by WLLN + continuous mapping theorem; hence $\widehat{\theta}_n$ is consistent.}

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt:
Use the delta method to find the asymptotic distribution of $\sqrt{n}(\widehat{\theta}_n - \theta)$. That is, give a result of the form
\(
\sqrt{n}(\widehat{\theta}_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \tau^2(\theta))
\)
and identify $\tau^2(\theta)$.
parts.modelAnswer:
By the Central Limit Theorem applied to $\bar{X}_n$ (with mean $1/\theta$ and variance $1/\theta^2$):
\[
\sqrt{n}\big(\bar{X}_n - 1/\theta\big) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1/\theta^2).
\]
Apply the delta method with $g(t) = 1/t$, so $g'(t) = -1/t^2$ and $g'(1/\theta) = -\theta^2$. Thus
\[
\sqrt{n}\big(\widehat{\theta}_n - \theta\big) = \sqrt{n}\big(g(\bar{X}_n) - g(1/\theta)\big) \xrightarrow{d} N\!\big(0,\, [g'(1/\theta)]^2 \cdot 1/\theta^2\big) = N(0,\, \theta^4 \cdot 1/\theta^2) = N(0, \theta^2).
\]

**Answer.** $\sqrt{n}(\widehat{\theta}_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \theta^2)$, i.e.\ $\tau^2(\theta) = \theta^2$.}

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Using the result from (c), construct an approximate $95\%$ confidence interval for $\theta$. (You may plug in $\widehat{\theta}_n$ for $\theta$ in the asymptotic variance --- justify briefly why this is allowed.)
parts.modelAnswer:
From (c), for large $n$, $\widehat{\theta}_n \approx N(\theta, \theta^2/n)$. Replacing $\theta$ by the consistent estimator $\widehat{\theta}_n$ in the asymptotic standard error is justified by Slutsky's theorem (since $\widehat{\theta}_n / \theta \xrightarrow{P} 1$). An approximate $95\%$ CI for $\theta$ is therefore
\[
\widehat{\theta}_n \pm z_{0.025} \cdot \frac{\widehat{\theta}_n}{\sqrt{n}} = \widehat{\theta}_n \left(1 \pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{n}}\right).
\]

**Answer.** $\Big[\,\widehat{\theta}_n\big(1 - 1.96/\sqrt{n}\big),\; \widehat{\theta}_n\big(1 + 1.96/\sqrt{n}\big)\,\Big]$, where $\widehat{\theta}_n = 1/\bar{X}_n$.}

% --- B2 ---

problems.title: Bernoulli with a Beta Prior; Sufficiency
problems.prompt: Let $X_1, \dots, X_n$ be i.i.d.\ $\mathrm{Bernoulli}(p)$, where $p \in (0,1)$ is unknown. Suppose the prior on $p$ is $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ with known hyperparameters $\alpha, \beta > 0$.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the posterior distribution of $p$ given $X_1, \dots, X_n$ and identify it by name and parameters.
parts.modelAnswer:
The Beta$(\alpha, \beta)$ prior has density $\pi(p) \propto p^{\alpha - 1}(1 - p)^{\beta - 1}$ on $(0,1)$. By Bayes' rule,
\[
\pi(p \mid \mathbf{X}) \;\propto\; L(p)\,\pi(p) \;\propto\; p^{\alpha + t - 1}(1 - p)^{\beta + n - t - 1}.
\]
This is the kernel of a Beta distribution.

**Answer.** $p \mid \mathbf{X} \;\sim\; \mathrm{Beta}\big(\alpha + T,\; \beta + n - T\big)$, where $T = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i$.}

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Find the Bayes estimator of $p$ under squared-error loss.
parts.modelAnswer:
Under squared-error loss the Bayes estimator is the posterior mean. For a $\mathrm{Beta}(a, b)$ distribution the mean is $a/(a+b)$, so
\[
\widehat{p}_{\text{Bayes}} = \frac{\alpha + T}{\alpha + \beta + n}.
\]

**Answer.** $\widehat{p}_{\text{Bayes}} = (\alpha + \sum X_i)/(\alpha + \beta + n)$.}

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Using the factorization criterion, show that $T = \sum_{i=1}^n X_i$ is a sufficient statistic for $p$.
parts.modelAnswer:
The joint pmf of $(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ is
\[
f_n(\mathbf{x} \mid p) = \prod_{i=1}^n p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1 - x_i} = p^{\,t}(1-p)^{n - t}, \qquad t = \textstyle\sum x_i,
\]
where each $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$. Take
\[
u(\mathbf{x}) = 1 \cdot \mathbf{1}\{x_i \in \{0,1\} \text{ for all } i\}, \qquad v(t, p) = p^t (1-p)^{n - t}.
\]
Then $f_n(\mathbf{x} \mid p) = u(\mathbf{x}) \cdot v(T(\mathbf{x}), p)$, which is the factorization criterion.

**Answer.** $T = \sum X_i$ is sufficient for $p$ by factorization with $u \equiv 1$ and $v(t, p) = p^t(1-p)^{n-t}$.}

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt:
What does the Bayes estimator from (b) converge to as $n \to \infty$? Justify briefly.

% =====================================================================
% PART C: Major Problems
% =====================================================================
\section*{Part C --- Major Problems \hfill (60 points total)}
parts.modelAnswer:
Rewrite the Bayes estimator:
\[
\widehat{p}_{\text{Bayes}} = \frac{\alpha + n \bar{X}_n}{\alpha + \beta + n}
= \frac{n}{\alpha + \beta + n}\,\bar{X}_n + \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta + n}.
\]
As $n \to \infty$, the first coefficient tends to $1$, the second to $0$, and $\bar{X}_n \xrightarrow{P} p$ by the WLLN. Hence (using continuous mapping / Slutsky):
\[
\widehat{p}_{\text{Bayes}} \;\xrightarrow{P}\; p.
\]
The data dominates the prior asymptotically; equivalently, the Bayes estimator converges to the MLE $\bar{X}_n$.

**Answer.** $\widehat{p}_{\text{Bayes}} \xrightarrow{P} p$ as $n \to \infty$ (the prior washes out; estimator $\to$ MLE).}

% =====================================================================
% PART C
% =====================================================================
\section*{Part C --- Major Problems}

problems.title: Two-Sample $t$-Test and $F$-Test
problems.prompt:
A researcher collects two independent samples assumed to follow normal distributions. Specifically:
\[
X_1, \dots, X_m \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu_X, \sigma^2), \qquad Y_1, \dots, Y_n \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu_Y, \sigma^2),
\]
with $m = 8$ and $n = 8$. The two samples are independent. The observed summary statistics are
\[
\bar{X}_m = 23.5, \qquad \bar{Y}_n = 21.0, \qquad S_X^2 = \sum_{i=1}^m (X_i - \bar{X}_m)^2 = 21, \qquad S_Y^2 = \sum_{j=1}^n (Y_j - \bar{Y}_n)^2 = 14.
\]

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt:
Test $H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$ versus $H_1: \mu_X \neq \mu_Y$ at significance level $\alpha_0 = 0.05$, assuming the common-variance model above. Specifically:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*),leftmargin=*]
  \item Write down the test statistic $U_{m,n}$ and its distribution under $H_0$.
  \item Compute the observed value of $U_{m,n}$ from the data.
  \item State the critical value, the rejection rule, and your decision.
\end{enumerate}
parts.modelAnswer:
**(i) Test statistic.** Under the common-variance model, the two-sample $t$-statistic is
\[
U_{m,n} = \frac{\sqrt{m + n - 2}\,(\bar{X} - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\big(\tfrac{1}{m} + \tfrac{1}{n}\big)\,(S_X^2 + S_Y^2)}}.
\]
Under $H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$, $U_{m,n} \sim t_{m + n - 2} = t_{14}$.

**(ii) Plug in.** Numerator: $\sqrt{14}\,(23.5 - 21.0) = 2.5\sqrt{14}$. Denominator: $\sqrt{(\tfrac{1}{8} + \tfrac{1}{8})(21 + 14)} = \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{4} \cdot 35} = \sqrt{35/4} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{35}$. Therefore
\[
U_{m,n} = \frac{2.5\sqrt{14}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{35}} = 5\sqrt{14/35} = 5\sqrt{2/5} = \sqrt{25 \cdot 2/5} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162.
\]

**(iii) Decision.** For a two-sided level-$0.05$ test, reject if $|U_{m,n}| > t_{0.025, 14} = 2.145$. Since $|\sqrt{10}| \approx 3.162 > 2.145$, we reject $H_0$.

**Answer.** $U_{m,n} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162$. Critical value $t_{0.025,14} = 2.145$. **Reject $H_0$** at $\alpha_0 = 0.05$: there is significant evidence that $\mu_X \neq \mu_Y$.}

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Construct a $95\%$ confidence interval for $\mu_X - \mu_Y$ (still under the common-variance model).
parts.modelAnswer:
The pooled variance estimator is
\[
\widehat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{S_X^2 + S_Y^2}{m + n - 2} = \frac{35}{14} = 2.5.
\]
The standard error of $\bar{X} - \bar{Y}$ is
\[
\mathrm{SE}(\bar{X} - \bar{Y}) = \sqrt{\widehat{\sigma}^2\big(\tfrac{1}{m} + \tfrac{1}{n}\big)} = \sqrt{2.5 \cdot \tfrac{1}{4}} = \sqrt{0.625} = \tfrac{\sqrt{10}}{4}.
\]
A $95\%$ CI for $\mu_X - \mu_Y$ is therefore
\[
(\bar{X} - \bar{Y}) \pm t_{0.025, 14} \cdot \mathrm{SE} = 2.5 \pm 2.145 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{10}}{4}.
\]

Numerically, $2.145 \cdot \sqrt{10}/4 \approx 2.145 \cdot 0.7906 \approx 1.696$.

**Answer.** $95\%$ CI for $\mu_X - \mu_Y$: $\big[\,2.5 - 2.145 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{10}}{4},\; 2.5 + 2.145 \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{10}}{4}\,\big] \approx [0.804,\, 4.196]$. Note $0$ is not in this interval, consistent with rejecting $H_0$ in part (a).}

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt:
Now suppose the researcher questions the common-variance assumption. Set up and carry out a two-sided test of
\[
H_0: \sigma_X^2 = \sigma_Y^2 \qquad \text{versus} \qquad H_1: \sigma_X^2 \neq \sigma_Y^2
\]
at level $\alpha_0 = 0.10$. Give the test statistic, its null distribution, the rejection region, and your decision. (Use the values of $S_X^2$ and $S_Y^2$ from above.)
parts.modelAnswer:
The $F$-statistic for comparing variances is
\[
V_{m,n} = \frac{S_X^2/(m - 1)}{S_Y^2/(n - 1)} = \frac{21/7}{14/7} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5.
\]
Under $H_0: \sigma_X^2 = \sigma_Y^2$, $V_{m,n} \sim F_{m-1, n-1} = F_{7, 7}$.

For a two-sided level-$\alpha_0 = 0.10$ test, reject if
\[
V \leq F_{0.95, 7, 7} = 1/F_{0.05, 7, 7} = 1/3.79 \approx 0.264 \quad \text{or} \quad V \geq F_{0.05, 7, 7} = 3.79.
\]
Since $0.264 < 1.5 < 3.79$, we fail to reject $H_0$.

**Answer.** $V = 1.5$, rejection region $V \leq 0.264$ or $V \geq 3.79$. **Fail to reject $H_0$** at $\alpha_0 = 0.10$: no significant evidence that $\sigma_X^2 \neq \sigma_Y^2$. The common-variance assumption used in (a)--(b) is not contradicted by the data.}

% --- C2 ---

problems.title: Neyman--Pearson Test, Power, and Sample Size
problems.prompt:
Let $X_1, \dots, X_n$ be i.i.d.\ $N(\theta, 1)$, with $\theta$ unknown. Consider the simple-versus-simple testing problem
\[
H_0: \theta = 0 \quad \text{versus} \quad H_1: \theta = 1
\]
at significance level $\alpha_0 = 0.05$. Take $n = 16$.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Apply the Neyman--Pearson Lemma to derive the most powerful test of $H_0$ versus $H_1$ at level $\alpha_0$. Show that the rejection region can be written in the form $\{\bar{X}_n > c\}$ for some constant $c$.
parts.modelAnswer:
The joint density under $\theta$ is
\[
f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = (2\pi)^{-n/2} \exp\!\Big(-\tfrac{1}{2}\textstyle\sum_i (x_i - \theta)^2\Big).
\]
The likelihood ratio is
\[
\frac{f_1(\mathbf{x})}{f_0(\mathbf{x})} = \exp\!\Big(-\tfrac{1}{2}\textstyle\sum_i [(x_i - 1)^2 - x_i^2]\Big) = \exp\!\Big(\textstyle\sum_i x_i - \tfrac{n}{2}\Big) = \exp\!\big(n \bar{x} - \tfrac{n}{2}\big).
\]
By the Neyman--Pearson Lemma, the most powerful level-$\alpha_0$ test rejects $H_0$ when $f_1/f_0 > k$ for some $k > 0$. Since $\exp(n\bar{x} - n/2)$ is strictly increasing in $\bar{x}$, this is equivalent to rejecting when
\[
\bar{X}_n > c \qquad \text{for some constant } c.
\]

The constant $c$ is chosen so that $P_{\theta = 0}(\bar{X}_n > c) = \alpha_0$.

**Answer.** The MP test rejects when $\bar{X}_n > c$, with $c$ chosen to make the size $= \alpha_0$.}

parts.label: (b)
parts.modelAnswer:
Under $H_0$ ($\theta = 0$), $\bar{X}_n \sim N(0, 1/n)$, so $\sqrt{n}\,\bar{X}_n \sim N(0,1)$. We need
\[
P_0(\bar{X}_n > c) = P\big(Z > \sqrt{n}\,c\big) = 0.05 \;\Longrightarrow\; \sqrt{n}\,c = z_{0.05} = 1.645.
\]
With $n = 16$, $\sqrt{n} = 4$, so $c = 1.645/4$.

**Answer.** $c = 1.645/4 \approx 0.4113$.}

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt: Compute the power $\pi(1) = P_{\theta=1}(\text{reject } H_0)$ of this test, expressed in terms of $\Phi$, the standard normal cdf. Then state the corresponding probability of Type II error $\beta$.
parts.modelAnswer:
Under $\theta = 1$, $\bar{X}_n \sim N(1, 1/n)$, so $\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}_n - 1) \sim N(0, 1)$. The power is
\[
\pi(1) = P_{\theta = 1}\big(\bar{X}_n > c\big) = P\!\left(Z > \sqrt{n}(c - 1)\right) = 1 - \Phi\big(\sqrt{n}\,c - \sqrt{n}\big) = \Phi\big(\sqrt{n} - \sqrt{n}\,c\big).
\]
Since $\sqrt{n}\,c = z_{0.05} = 1.645$ and $\sqrt{n} = 4$,
\[
\pi(1) = \Phi(4 - 1.645) = \Phi(2.355).
\]
The Type II error probability is $\beta = 1 - \pi(1) = 1 - \Phi(2.355)$.

Numerically $\Phi(2.355) \approx 0.9907$ (between $\Phi(2.326) = 0.99$ and $\Phi(2.576) = 0.995$), so $\beta \approx 0.0093$.

**Answer.** $\pi(1) = \Phi(4 - 1.645) = \Phi(2.355) \approx 0.9907$. \quad $\beta = 1 - \pi(1) \approx 0.0093$.}

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Using a level-$\alpha_0 = 0.05$ test of the same form as in (a), what is the smallest sample size $n^*$ such that the power against the alternative $\theta = 1$ satisfies $\pi(1) \geq 0.99$?
parts.modelAnswer:
For the analogous test with sample size $n$, the rejection threshold is $c_n = z_{0.05}/\sqrt{n}$ and the power is
\[
\pi(1; n) = \Phi\big(\sqrt{n} - z_{0.05}\big) = \Phi(\sqrt{n} - 1.645).
\]
We need $\pi(1; n) \geq 0.99 = \Phi(z_{0.01})$, i.e.\ $\sqrt{n} - 1.645 \geq z_{0.01} = 2.326$, hence
\[
\sqrt{n} \geq 1.645 + 2.326 = 3.971 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad n \geq 3.971^2 \approx 15.77.
\]
The smallest integer $n$ satisfying this is $n^* = 16$. (The current sample size already meets the $0.99$ power requirement, consistent with $\pi(1) \approx 0.9907$ from part (c).)

**Answer.** $n^* = 16$.}

% --- C3 ---

problems.title: MLE, Fisher Information, and Likelihood Ratio Test for Poisson
problems.prompt: Let $X_1, \dots, X_n$ be i.i.d.\ $\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)$, where $\lambda > 0$ is unknown. Recall the pmf $f(x \mid \lambda) = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x / x!$ for $x = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Find the MLE $\widehat{\lambda}_n$ of $\lambda$ and show that it is unbiased for $\lambda$.
parts.modelAnswer:
The log-likelihood (dropping terms free of $\lambda$) is
\[
\ell_n(\lambda) = -n\lambda + \log(\lambda) \sum_{i=1}^n X_i + \text{const}.
\]
Differentiating: $\dot{\ell}_n(\lambda) = -n + (\sum X_i)/\lambda$. Setting $= 0$ gives $\lambda = \bar{X}_n$. The second derivative $-(\sum X_i)/\lambda^2 < 0$, confirming a maximum.

**Unbiasedness:** $E_\lambda[\widehat{\lambda}_n] = E_\lambda[\bar{X}_n] = \tfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n E_\lambda[X_i] = \tfrac{1}{n}(n\lambda) = \lambda$.

**Answer.** $\widehat{\lambda}_n = \bar{X}_n$. It is unbiased: $E_\lambda[\widehat{\lambda}_n] = \lambda$.}

parts.label: (b)
parts.prompt: Compute the Fisher information $I(\lambda)$ in a single observation, and the Fisher information $I_n(\lambda)$ in the full sample. Use the Cram\'er--Rao Lower Bound to determine whether $\widehat{\lambda}_n$ is efficient.
parts.modelAnswer:
For a single observation $X \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)$:
$\log f(x;\lambda) = -\lambda + x \log \lambda - \log(x!)$, so the score is $\dot{\ell}(x;\lambda) = -1 + x/\lambda$. The Fisher information is
\[
I(\lambda) = E_\lambda\!\left[\dot{\ell}(X;\lambda)^2\right] = \mathrm{Var}_\lambda(\dot{\ell}) = \mathrm{Var}_\lambda(X/\lambda) = \frac{\mathrm{Var}_\lambda(X)}{\lambda^2} = \frac{\lambda}{\lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\lambda}.
\]
For the full sample (i.i.d.): $I_n(\lambda) = n I(\lambda) = n/\lambda$.

**Efficiency check.** The CRLB for unbiased estimators of $\lambda$ is $1/[n I(\lambda)] = \lambda/n$. The variance of $\widehat{\lambda}_n$ is
\[
\mathrm{Var}_\lambda(\widehat{\lambda}_n) = \mathrm{Var}_\lambda(\bar{X}_n) = \frac{\mathrm{Var}_\lambda(X_1)}{n} = \frac{\lambda}{n}.
\]
This equals the CRLB, so $\widehat{\lambda}_n$ achieves the bound and is therefore efficient.

**Answer.** $I(\lambda) = 1/\lambda$, $\;I_n(\lambda) = n/\lambda$, $\;$CRLB $= \lambda/n = \mathrm{Var}_\lambda(\widehat{\lambda}_n)$. Yes, $\widehat{\lambda}_n$ is efficient.}

parts.label: (c)
parts.prompt:
Derive the likelihood ratio test statistic $\Lambda(\mathbf{X})$ for testing
\[
H_0: \lambda = \lambda_0 \quad \text{versus} \quad H_1: \lambda \neq \lambda_0,
\]
where $\lambda_0 > 0$ is a fixed value. Simplify $-2 \log \Lambda(\mathbf{X})$ as much as possible (your answer should be in terms of $n$, $\widehat{\lambda}_n$, and $\lambda_0$).
parts.modelAnswer:
The likelihood is (constant $C(\mathbf{x}) = 1/\prod x_i!$)
\[
L_n(\lambda; \mathbf{x}) = e^{-n\lambda} \lambda^{\,n\bar{x}} \cdot C(\mathbf{x}).
\]
Under $H_0$ the supremum is $L_n(\lambda_0)$. Over all of $\Omega$ the supremum is $L_n(\widehat{\lambda}_n) = L_n(\bar{x})$. Therefore
\[
\Lambda(\mathbf{X}) = \frac{L_n(\lambda_0)}{L_n(\widehat{\lambda}_n)} = \frac{e^{-n\lambda_0} \lambda_0^{\,n\widehat{\lambda}_n}}{e^{-n\widehat{\lambda}_n} \widehat{\lambda}_n^{\,n\widehat{\lambda}_n}} = \exp\!\Big(-n(\lambda_0 - \widehat{\lambda}_n)\Big) \left(\frac{\lambda_0}{\widehat{\lambda}_n}\right)^{n\widehat{\lambda}_n}.
\]
Take logs:
\[
\log\Lambda = -n(\lambda_0 - \widehat{\lambda}_n) + n\widehat{\lambda}_n \log(\lambda_0/\widehat{\lambda}_n) = n(\widehat{\lambda}_n - \lambda_0) - n\widehat{\lambda}_n \log(\widehat{\lambda}_n/\lambda_0).
\]
Therefore
\[
-2 \log \Lambda(\mathbf{X}) = 2n\!\left[\widehat{\lambda}_n \log\!\frac{\widehat{\lambda}_n}{\lambda_0} \;-\; \big(\widehat{\lambda}_n - \lambda_0\big)\right].
\]

**Answer.** $\Lambda(\mathbf{X}) = e^{-n(\lambda_0 - \widehat{\lambda}_n)}\,(\lambda_0/\widehat{\lambda}_n)^{n\widehat{\lambda}_n}$. Equivalently, $-2\log\Lambda(\mathbf{X}) = 2n\big[\widehat{\lambda}_n \log(\widehat{\lambda}_n/\lambda_0) - (\widehat{\lambda}_n - \lambda_0)\big]$.}

parts.label: (d)
parts.prompt: Using Wilks' theorem, give an asymptotic level-$\alpha_0 = 0.05$ rejection rule based on $-2\log\Lambda(\mathbf{X})$. State the limiting distribution clearly.
parts.modelAnswer:
Wilks' theorem: under $H_0$ with $H_0$ specifying $k = 1$ coordinate, as $n \to \infty$,
\[
-2 \log \Lambda(\mathbf{X}) \;\xrightarrow{d}\; \chi^2_1.
\]
A level-$\alpha_0 = 0.05$ asymptotic test therefore rejects $H_0$ when
\[
-2\log\Lambda(\mathbf{X}) \;>\; \chi^2_{0.05, 1} = 3.841.
\]

**Answer.** Reject $H_0$ at asymptotic level $0.05$ when $-2\log\Lambda(\mathbf{X}) > 3.841$. (Limiting distribution: $\chi^2_1$.)}

_End of Solutions --- Total Points: $10 + 30 + 60 = 100$._
